speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Like the propaganda got the people down on the recon. Or the North Koreans, you know how they do it, the Germans, I guess the Germans were nothing but pigs. Oh, they were, it wasn't even human beings, you know how the propaganda goes. Why it was almost, to shoot a German one time, it was just like squatting. You think more about going out and shooting a digong, look at that. You know, they just, how the propaganda can get people down, well that's the way it was from the Wobbly Museum. They just, why, all the newspapers were full of it, and just, why does the most terrible thing ever happen to a country? They'd hear the newspapers. Like I say, they didn't even have no chat organization. I don't know the secret, I couldn't get no dope on it, I didn't know who the heck was who. And they didn't know the higher man, whether they were higher than Wobbly, or whether they wasn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like in the community, like around Princeton here, among the families that lived, was there real hard feelings one way or the other?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Towards the Wobbly's?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, were the families either going to be sort of pro or against, or did people keep shut about it and not want to say anything for fear that they'd be thought of?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: It was quiet, yes, but there was a lot of antagonism, because like I say, you know, some farmers' foods had a lot of them burn up. Well, it didn't make no difference, somebody threw the cigarette out there accidentally and started a fire, but it was automatically in the wall, it used to burn that field up. Well, you know how it goes in wheat fields going up around the country in the trash machines, another trick they do, bad guys should put dynamite in wheat bundles, and bundles of grain. Only when they throw it in the damn machine, why they got the dynamite, you know, it'd hit them cinders at the price of wheat. Bang, throw the damn thing sky high. It was hell, and they put spikes in the grain, and put a nail in the daggone bundle. Of course, when that hit the daggone cinders, it wouldn't blow up anything, but it sure wrecked the damn thing. And that was the way of getting these darn guys working, guys 12 hours a day, bad guys, for a dollar a day or something, just almost nothing. Bad guys, when a few cylinders went out on the farm, by the time they started thinking about it, just drilled them back up to where they started paying more money and running shorter hours, they cut them 12 hours down to 10 hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it had an effect on the farming country too? No, it didn't. Everything.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Everything that was working them in for nothing, and giving them no living quarters, you know, just terrible the way that working man had done in the early days. Just terrible. Couldn't give them more, a lot of farmers would give a dog a better place to sleep by, guys, they'd give them the working man. Most of them slept in the barn. That's the only bunkhouse they had, was out in the barn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much difference between the farmers now, among the farmers, were there some farms around here that could have afforded better to the men and other farms that were just dirt poor farms, or what was the difference among the farms in the old days?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, the farmers could have afforded them better, just like everything else, you know, if they can get a man to work for nothing, they'll do it. There's no laws, if a man didn't work quite hard enough to do the farming, I set him on down the road talking to himself and get another guy. I don't know, just a lot of hard feelings between a working man, and I don't think the farmers felt bad off, though, as the farmer himself. It was a, see the whole country used to have big stationary machines they'd pull around the country thrice and drain, you know. Them guys, they got hard on them guys, but a lot of machines got rim racked. Some farmers, they got into a farmer, I guess, you might see a barn go up in smoke, and there's a wobbly dump, the barn still burned up, there's no wobbly's in that country, but the barn's burned up, most of the time it tears me. There ain't no wobbly's to play with on this time. I'll quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Movement was a prime form of play for youngsters in the early days, on bicycles, horses, sleds and skates, and later on motorcycles, Emmett Utte tells of a boy's adventures in the Princeton country. Then he describes his grown-up work as a sawyer on the Potlatch mill.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Maybe we had to warm up more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, maybe we talked to warm up.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, you got it all hooked up now. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, don't worry about it, I just want to start talking about the old times.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah. Well, I don't know, old timers, there's so many of them, I ain't here no more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah. Seems like there's a lot more that are gone than are still here.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, you got the Tipperillos.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I got a Tipperillos. A little bit of back. Don't got it matched though.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You got it matched? Well, maybe I can come up with one. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted you to tell me some more stories about when you were a kid. I wanted you to tell me about stories that happened to you when you were just a boy.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, I don't know, it's like a lot different than the way we used to live and do nowadays. I guess, you know, we lived up on the hill up there. Oh, about a mile and a half above here, quite a hill up there. But, gee, you know, if I wanted something in Potlatch, I wanted to go get a haircut as a kid, I'd just go back. I would strike off and I'd run halfway to Princeton, take the railroad track and go to Potlatch, up to Potlatch. Walk back home and never think nothing about it. Thank gosh, you know, just good exercise. Now, you know, I got grandkids over here, they wouldn't, if you'd ask them to walk over to Princeton and get something, oh boy. They'd have to have a V8 and get over their back. No, that's no good. They just won't walk. But we didn't make nothing about it, you know. Just like grandkids, huh? But your neighbor's kids might have lived up here, they lived up here, they moved up to Elk River when they started building that town up. They started to put a sawmill in there and they started building it up. I got me a new bicycle that spring. And so, by gosh, I could hardly wait until the roads got dry enough up in the hills, you know. It stays muddy up there and I used to be in jungle, you know, all the way up. By the time you get out of Harvard way, by gosh, you're in the jungle, clear to Elk River. Oh, you'd come in a little town like there, you know, but then you'd just come out of there and you'd be right back in the jungle again. You wouldn't believe it. It was like that now. Elk River, from Mobile to Elk River, it was just a great jungle all the way through there. Nobody lived there, I don't know, it was much than 20 miles or better through there. The old roads, you wouldn't notice it. The old roads used to stay up above the creek and you'd ride down a hill and walk up the next one. You'd push the bicycle over that and you'd ride down that and up the hill. I didn't take time, I was afraid I'd get caught up in that jungle in the dark. I'd been through there before with the folks when they used to go up there fishing and stuff, you know. They'd go up to Elk River, four horses on a wagon, camping up there. It would take a couple or three days to go to Elk River. I'd been through there before, but I didn't want to get caught in that jungle. I stopped and did, bumped the liddie for a glass of water. I should have stopped and got me something to eat. By the guys I was pretty, if I did, by the guys I'd be caught in the dark. I kept on hiking along while I got out of the boat a little ways and the old Model D-4s, that poor guy there, they passed me. And you know, clear from there to the Neva Mountain, they didn't get very far ahead of me. I'd get on the bike and I'd go down that hill and run up the next one just to heckin', you know. I got to the hill and I could still see the dust of that fort ahead of me. But they lost me when they hit the Neva Mountain there. Well, I just about ran out of steam back there, so I didn't have anything to eat. And I know there was a creek going down there, it was just running yellow. So there was mine up on the mountain above it, and that creek was just running yellow. And I walked that road going out along the creek there, oh, I just went and went on and on and on. Finally, I see the road make a turn and go across the bridge, then it started up the mountain. You could see it from this side, where you started up that mountain, that's what they call the Neva Mountain up there. And I thought, boy, I just got to have a drink. Did I tell you about that before?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't remember if we put it on tape or not.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Anyway, I went down and I had to hold my nose to drink. I got down and I hold the yellow water and I took a swig of it. That made me feel better, an awful taste of water. But then I got out on a bike and I wheeled across the bridge, and towards the side of the mountain there was a sign that says fresh water. Well, it wasn't much fun, you know, as I tell you. Well, I drank that old sloppy water, that says fresh water, so I took another swig. And I had to wheel the bicycle all the way up that mountain. I got there pretty well halfway up the mountain and tried running across huckleberries. Well, by gosh, I just couldn't, I had to get something deep. So I stopped and beat huckleberries to beat the devil, and I go further up the mountain, beat some more Huckleberries. Finally got pretty well on top of the mountain, a bunch of cars parked there, they left at huckleberries. It was the first time I ever bumped anything in my life, so I knew I wasn't broke. I asked the guy when I got to the heading of length left over, he was down at the car getting ready to go home. I asked him if I had any length left over, and I had the guy's nose bag out and I walked and I found a couple of sandwiches. Oh, did you pay him for it? No, heck, he said it's all right. He was amazed when he found out where I'd come from. It was a bicycle. So anyway, that was quite a shot in the arm, I got something in his tummy. Then after you get to the top of the mountain, pretty well downgraded most of the way down to huckleberries. I had three wheeling bags, I got top of that mountain going down to the other side, I really went. Well, I got in down to where I had lots of time, you know, it was a long time in the summertime, long time for dark, yeah. I got in there around four o'clock, something like that. I got something, that was a long ride. I didn't make too bad a time, just dirt roads, dust and dirt, you know, no gravel, all the way up, no dirt roads, all dirt roads all the way. I don't know what time I left home that morning, but like I said, it was about four o'clock afternoon and I threw it up here with a bicycle. I got something to do about the time. I went to do about the time when we figured it up and down hills. Nowadays, all together different, now you go out of Bovill there and the bad guys just water the grades, you know, all the way up and leave a mountain. They got that cut down, now they cut the whole top of it down, the bad guys to, well just little grades now, I think I felt like I could ride a bike right over it. It's not steep like it used to be, heck it used to just turn right up the mountain and go up over the top of the old road. I don't know, kids won't do them things nowadays, us kids, a bunch of us had bikes around here. Any Sunday, a bunch of us, four, five, six of us take off and, well, where are we going? Well, go to Palouse, maybe Garfield, Moscow. We'd drive the Moscow back in a day, I believe. Never thought nothing about it nowadays, that's a long ways for a motorcycle, these kids. Kids got motorcycles to talk about driving the Moscow back. That's a long ways. This kid, he's riding an old bike, single speeds, didn't have a 10 speed bike Sunday. Although, the kid did have a three speed, I don't know, he lived up above here. He had a Chief, got a seven row bike, I think, a seven row bike Chief, three speed Chief. And he got one of them, I had an old Ranger, single speed, down here on this little hill out here. Well, it's cut down now a lot, what it was, you know, they got pretty steep banks up on the side up there, now they cut it down. It used to be you'd go up there a ways, then all the ones would just turn up and over the head, you know. Well, he had his load gear, second gear, high gear. So we got into an argument like kids do. And I was pretty stout on my legs up there. Anyway, I told him by the guys I could out pull him up that hill, so we got up there, that's where the hill turned up. I said, no, it was stuck from here and pedaled over that hill. You know, he couldn't do it. He was kind of a spinning leg little devil, he wasn't too stout. But he had a load gear on it, and he still couldn't cut the musters. But he could outrun me when he put that thing on high gear though. He could take off and lose it. Thirty-five mile an hour by the way, as far as you could pedal it, that old single speed. I know my dad, over here on the road, used to go cross country here. It's down where it doesn't now, it used to turn right down here at the barn, go right across. And I was up on that straightaway, and my dad was in the car. And so he, I pedaled, as far as I could pedal, and he drove along. Thirty-five mile an hour, as far as I could go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's a pretty darn fast thing.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I was pedaling right along, isn't that right? I was really pointed on, about thirty-five mile an hour, according to the speedometer on that old car. I don't know how I could type like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Once you got a bicycle, did you pretty much use a bike instead of run?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh yeah, yeah. Yeah, it didn't make no difference. I had to walk, I would walk. I had a bike right there. Usually I'd have a couple saddle horses all the time, you know. In the wintertime I'd ride the saddle horses everywhere I went. In the summertime we'd turn the horses out on the range, up there back when Back Hills back here is what they used to call the range. Putting the old farm ground down. It used to be a pretty country back there, nobody lived back there. And we'd talk the range while they turned the horses out. And probably wouldn't get the horses up until the time the schools started. The kids used to drive a little little buggy, signal shaves it, you know, signal the horses out. And in the wintertime, in the snowgum, we'd take that body off the buggy and put it on a little cutter. Just a cutter with runners on it, you know, you'd suck the body off of that onto the cutter. And had a little horse shade again in the wintertime. Oh, I don't know. I wouldn't trade my life those days, even if it was kind of dull. Kids nowadays would be awful dull life for kids nowadays, but damn it, we used to have fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sounds like there's more excitement for me.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, it... I don't know, it just... It makes you awful disgusted what young people nowadays are the way they do. The way we used to have to do, but we didn't have no choice. There was no cars in the country when I was old, and I got about 12, 13 years old, and cars started coming in. One old guy over here at Princeton, old bachelor over there, he had the first car hit the town, the old EM&F. I don't know what the EM&F stands for. They used to nickname it, Every Morning Fixin'. That's what they used to call him, Every Morning Fixin'. That's about what it's about. He'd work on it all week, buy a guy, just take it out and drive it one time, come back and work on it another week. EM&F. I didn't know what that stood for, EM&F. They nicknamed it, Every Morning Fixin'.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a very good mechanic, this guy?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He must have been the first mechanic in the country.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I suppose he had to be some half-assed mechanic, I don't know. They didn't have any garages around them days, so I guess you just haywire him up and go. So anyway, he had the first car, and there used to be a guy over here, I'm trying to get him a livery bar. He had driving horses all the time. Well, he had workhorses, you know, Hall Freight and Clueset, you know, before the railroad come in looking. Hall Freight up in Clues, and then he wanted to go to Clues. Pay a fair price to either drive your own horses or the driver would take you. Oh boy, they used to have some horses. I know he had a little driving team. Boy, they could just take off and make them old buggy tires just saying, you know... Sure, had some good driving horses them days. But anyway, finally you got a Model D Ford. The whole family's back and forth here. That's about the second car coming in the country, I think. Oh, people used to be crazy about cars, you know. People driving miles, you know, before they used to see a car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No fooling.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know when the first car they ever saw by Gosh was Clues. Some guy down there, I don't know how he owned the car. It had it in there, a different brick than the one. I don't know if the folks drove horses to see the Clues by Gosh, just to see that thing come. And when Gosh stood back up in 1929, Dodge come out with the first six cylinder car. They called it a Dodge Victory Six. And there's always been four zombies before, you know, and they'd come out with it. Well, the Jews got a hold of the Dodge Outfit and they'd come out with a Dodge Victory Six. And oh, they advertised it and advertised it. They was going to have one in Spokane. And I didn't go up with them. You'd be surprised how many people drove through Spokane to see that new Dodge. It was the biggest lemon ever put out. And a friend of mine from Moscow, Don Williams, he used to work in post office over there. He bought one of them. Oh man, that was a lemon. It sure was. See, Dodge, when they had the old Porsche, Dodge Brothers, when they had it, they put out them old Porsche and the Dodge's. They just couldn't beat them. They wasn't too bad on the highway, on the roads, and then the mountains. They couldn't get there with the Dodge. They were just trying to do anything else, you know. And then the Jews got a hold of the Dodge Brothers. They didn't call it Dodge Brothers anymore, it was just Dodge. They got the name of Dodge when they got away with the brothers. Well, first thing they done, they put out the fastest Ford in America. That was the next car after the old Dodge Brothers. Fastest Ford in America. And still the Ford's second car, yes. But they cheapened it down. The body wasn't too good on it. It didn't last long. That was the 28th. Then the 29th, they come out with that victory six. And that was the first sixth of the car they come out with. That was the real limit. And then Dodge kind of lost its reputation for, oh, up in the 30's some place they come out with it. Well, Chrysler took it over. Chrysler went out there and the Jews, Chrysler went over there and they started putting out better cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the cars came in real slow, but first there was only one and then the...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: In the thick of the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, well that's what I'm thinking of. Yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: It all went, oh about 19, well it was about 1914. Then there was Tom Dick and Harry Pritger had a car, you know. Anybody could afford one had a car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was the first car, this first guy?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I think it was about 1910, 1911 in there. That old guy had an OEM and F. And I don't know about 1913 I think when Ira Craig got one. But then they got the mass produced and the darn things. And oh, I don't know, my dad had his first car in 1970 really.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me you traded six horses with down payment on this first car.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, I traded horses with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most people go like that, trade horses for down payments? Was it rough to afford a car around here when that car was coming in?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, people didn't have much money and no much cash. I don't know if you had horses or cows why they'd take them for down payment. They'd take them for the old payment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Everybody wanted them at once. Everybody thought it was a great thing.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah. Well, they'll know it. It was funny, my guys, when they first come out, I know there used to be an old man Hawkins over here, running a hardware store in Hampton, Princeton. Probably named Lee Beth had an agency that supported Beth Luce. So he come up and sold him a Model D board. And oh, I don't know, someplace along the highway, I was hiding and bragging on it, you could turn it on a dime. You know, turn it around on a dime. So that old road just run around Donnerly over there. They got over there and there was just a little wide place on the road where they cut the bank off. And so he decided to turn back or something instead of going down here and backing up. He just went over next to the bank and turned around. He went out of there and got feather down over the bank. And he's going to sue Beth because he told me, by gosh, you could turn it on a dime. He said that spot was a lot bigger than a dime. That's right. College. College full of things used to happen. All for it, by gosh. You can have any color you want, as long as it's black. They only put them out when they got there black. It was a joke about them. You can have any color you want, as long as it's black. They used to be a dingle dying of friends, old dingle mics. You'd get a Ford engineer every couple years. He'd get it home and paint it yellow. I thought, oh, why? He'd take them and paint them a brand new kind. He'd take them and paint them yellow. You'd always tell old dingle mics when you see them coming by, you'd say, yellow Ford.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd say, yellow Ford.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, you'd love this. Tell old dingle mics.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did the livery stay with horses?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, anyway, they went out. They got to putting trucks out. They built a railroad up. Well, that went away from Brighton, from Toulouse. Yeah, they built a road up in...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The railroad was in 06, something like that.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, they built a mill down here in 1906. But they built a road from... ...to Potlatch. And then after they got that far, well, then later on they went on up the line with it. I think they'd run for Harvard. Next thing they linked onto it was Harvard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then they...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: When I was a pretty good-sized kid, I went on from Harvard on up the line. I know a folk drove up there on a picnic one day. They went up there to watch. They had the big steam shovel working, ditcher. They had on a black car, you know, and the train, the old work train pushing us. Well, they'd go up there in them cuts and that shovel would dig the damn thing out and whirl around and dump the dirt back on them black cars. And that's the way they dug them cuts up there. Well, we was up there watching them dig up there. And they'd go up there and dig up there. You know where you go under that truffle? Just up on the hill, almost on top of that hill, the other side is where they were working, where we watched them. Big tunnels, cuts, but they were cut bigger. Boy, that was a big machine. Why was it that big around watching that big machine work?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I heard there were a lot of Italians, that a lot of Italians and Europeans that worked on the railroad crews.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: And the dig was, oh yeah, a lot of Italians. I know one time there was a kid who was living up here on the river up here, a little ways up. Almost about halfway to Harvard there, we was living there by the long side of the railroad tracks. And the diggers got killed down there. The train come down, they didn't hear it, it was too late and they tried to get their hang car off their track. Well, there was four or five of them there, but it got right down to Nippon Picking, where they all read from, and they took out the road. And those other guys thought if we could just give them one more heave, they could save us hang car. The train hit the hang car and killed it. And so sometime after that, a couple Italians, they come from the old country, we were brothers I think. And they come to our house wanting to know all about the details of it, how he got killed and everything. We had a heck of a time trying to tell them because they couldn't talk good English.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they pick your house? They picked your house to find out.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: We lived right there close to where it actually happened. We just found a little knoll there and then went through that tunnel below the house there. And then, oh, about a couple hundred yards above, that's where it actually happened. So I remember my dad, he went up and I went with him to show him where it happened up there. I don't know what good it does to see where it happened, but anyway, we took him up there and showed him where it happened. They seemed to be satisfied about their business, but they come through here from Midland. They just kept the details of it. So, oh yeah. Top wire. Top wire was just so high above the snow. I laid on that sled just as tight as I could lay up and tried to go slow under them wires and I'd hang up every time. I just couldn't go under them wires. Going slow and just laying, you know, like just bow-jiggling on the sled. I'd hang up on that wire every time I could try it. But I went through there coming down there like a bat out of heck. And I went through both them fences just like that and I never even drove a course. How do you explain that? I guess I was going so fast I stretched out or something. That's the truth, I guess. I went back up there and I was scared to death. I found what I'd done, you know, once I could have got my head cut off. And anyway, I seen that fence and I just flattened on that sled and nothing happened. So after I figured, well, I better get my head up and look and see where I am because I didn't want to raise it up in time to catch a bar of wire in the resource. So anyway, I went through down there half a quarter further by the guy that almost really hit a fence down there while I got up to look and see where I was at. Anyway, I went through around the corner and stopped and nothing happened. And I went over there and I tried my best to wear the same clothes and I just flattened myself on that sled the best I could do it. And I go down the stairs and I'd go through there slow and I'd hang up every time on that fence. And it's how you made those little hatches. Lord's watching you someplace. Lord's watching you. Lord took care of his kids, I guess. Anyway, I went through two fences, not just one, I went through two of them. I never even told my coach. So the answer to that was, well, there's nothing wrong with it. It wasn't only about that high bug that got down there in the snow, you know, because the snow had top wire. I didn't go over it. I had to go under it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like you ran around a lot when you were a kid. Did your parents put men in?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No wonder we ever lived over our food, since we're done riding, bucking horses. None of us was saddle-brunch. We had more, got some brains most of the time. I don't know. If more kids ever lived, certainly did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about ice skating? You told me you'd get ice skating stories.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah, well, you know, you see kids skate on ice, it's about all the mountains. We used to go and skate on the river. Well, I think I told you about that. That bug tried to pull a bathroom out of another kid and I.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's the one I wanted you to tell me again.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, that's the bathroom. Otherwise, it's just kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tell me that one again, though. I like that one.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, anyway, we was one of the younger kids, you know. So they always cooked up a plot about somebody getting a pick on somebody. So they took me and this kid down the river. This guy did way down the river and fooled around down there. And we were skating back up. We had a big bonfire over here just above the bridge up there. That's where everybody gathered. Oh, there used to be 35, 40 people gathered. They're nice, skate nice, you know. Dark. Get away from the bonfire a little ways and it's some dark. I don't know. It's just intuition happened. I don't know. Anyway, we was coming back up. While we were gone, there was a bunch of guys that took me out of the big hole in the ice. Took us to the place where we just skate. Oh, it must have been, why just near that lounge over there. Quite a joke. And I didn't know nothing about it. Of course, the guy took us down there with it. He knew what was going on. So we just got in a race coming back, the first one to get back to the bonfire. Well, this kid, he was just about nipping, touching, speed between us. Sometimes I beat him, sometimes I beat me. But this time he had the lead on me a little bit. But we was almost getting side by side. But the guy with us, before he got that hole, he cut off. Let the kid and I go, well, I don't know what happened. I couldn't see that. But something told me, and I gave a jump. And I jumped through that hole, and I had no mistakes. And went on, but the other kid, well, he wasn't even looking. He was skating every time out watching to see if I gained on him or not. In these cases, I didn't do anything. And you couldn't see it. It was dark and nice. It wasn't daylight. And then, I don't know what made me, right at the last minute, something hit me. And I said, I gave a big jump. I jumped through that hole, landed on the skates. And the other kid, I never could make him believe I wasn't in on that. He never liked me after that. He always held against me because he thought I was in on that to give him, that I wouldn't have jumped if I wasn't in on it. I could never explain to him, but that guy said, that was a trap for me to say it was him. I don't know what got me there to jump when I did. And when I did, he went into it. So he always blamed me for that. And I don't think he ever forgot it. I tried to explain it, but I guess it wasn't. Oh, I went down. I don't know. He was down at Pullman. And I don't know what. He was having trouble with his wife, too. But he was working there in the beer parlor. A friend of mine told him where he was working, so I went up there and went in and sat down there. He'd come up and wasn't what I wanted. And I thought maybe he'd recognize me, and he didn't. So finally I just up and told him who I was. Oh, he said, oh, yes, yes. He went ahead wiping off the counter just like I wasn't there. I got a minute on about my business. I don't know. And just one of them things you got no answer to. I don't know why he should feel that way. I hadn't seen him for years. He was the same one that was on the horse up there with me. I went and rode them cows that time. I was just driving with him a lot. I don't know. It just kind of gives you a funny feeling. I went in there, and I recognized him. He looked older and everything. I hadn't seen him for 30 years, I suppose. I went in there and I told him probably who I was. He just acknowledged it and went on about his work. So I got moped out. I haven't seen him since. Why would you do a case like that? Well, you could do, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There's nothing you can do.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I hated him like a devil. I don't know if he still held a grudge against me on the count of... ...on the count of ice business or not. Well, he was always in competition. He was the one I told you about. He had a three-speed bike. And I had the old single speed. I told him on that hill. There was always competition between him. He just wanted to seem like he never could have me get the best of him. He had to be better than I was or something. Always competition.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it just between you and him, or was it with him and everybody?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I can't answer for everybody else. I know it was between him and I that way. It sure seemed like he didn't want me to ever best him on anything. I know we had two horses riding him. I had a brand new saddle. He was riding bareback. He wanted to ride my saddle away, so he was coming down off the mountains up there. So I took him. I was riding bareback with him, riding my saddle. We'd always race. So anyway, we come down off the hill. We come down off this hill, went down, and there's a little hill here, and I'm down, and then up another hill. Well, we come down off this hill, he took off. Well, that little coyote I was riding, whenever another one took off, she was off too, you know. Well, it went around into a race. Well, he was ahead of me. And he was looking back to see if I had gained on him, just as he went down this hill and up over this. Well, what we didn't know was a bunch of cows laying on the down road just over this hill. Four, ten, fifteen head of cows laying there, and cows laying on dusty roads. They laid just all over the road there. Well, the quicker I come up over the hill, I seen them cows. But we didn't have far from it that road down here to go, you know. He was right among them. Well, he was whipping his horse and looking back to see if I was gaining on him, he went over this hill, and that horse jumped right among them cows, and the cows all started getting up about the same time. And so, help me, that horse went indoor in. Just turned a complete summer set among them cows. And, of course, it landed on its back on my new saddle. And it threw him off the side of the road there. Not to be malleable, he'd finally come through. But I wasn't supposed to tell his folks that he wasn't supposed to run that horse. And so I told him I wouldn't tell him. But he was pretty badly shaken up. So we had to fool around quite a while then before he went home. So he kind of got straightened up before he went home. Got his clothes dusted off. It hurt me worse because he skinned up my saddle. The horn, you know, and the cattle back there, you know. He had to get his horse and stuff in it on that saddle and knock the cargo leather off of the horn. Kind of scarred the leather on the, well, the toy leather a little bit on the cattle back there. That hurt me worse than him getting hurt, my new saddle getting skinned up. I don't know how he ever lived over that one. But, man, he was just whipping his horse and looking back at him, his tongue hanging out, and he started going, and went over that hill and there was a cow laying all over the road. Talk about a commotion. And that horse just turned a complete summer scent. And how that kid among them cows and everything, he didn't get hurt worse than he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about that story that you told me about riding a motorcycle up and down the tracks that time? That's another somersault story.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, it wasn't on the tracks on the main road doing it into the sawmill down here. I guess that's the only one I can think of. When I, we was out running the train?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, that's the one I knew.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, I told you that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, but why did you tell me that one again, too? None of these I bothered to put down before.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, anyway, we come out of the mill, it was noon hour, and we was both going up, and we both lived up in town at that time down at Potlatch. We'd go home and dinner. Well, I was riding the motorcycle, and he'd been usually riding with me all the time, going up and coming back to work at noon hour. So we got out there and just about ready to crank the old motor up by the guys, and the train was coming back and down and switching. And switching back down, we were going to go out across the crossing where we had to go to get uptown. Anyway, he said, here comes the darn train, and he said we're going to be held up. I took a look at it, and it was quite a long so far up the line before the bus was crossing yet, and we were coming down fairly slow, but moving right along. I could give the old motorcycle a kick over, and if I didn't jump on, we'd beat the damn thing. So anyway, I took off, we had to do about 70, 75 in second gear. And we took off, and it was only about a quarter of a mile, I guess, up the tracks. Well, everything was going all right. I was out running the train, but about 100 yards before I got to the tracks, the damn little hound dog came out from under that walk, a board walker used to walk up to him and walk back and forth on going to work. Come up on that walk and start across the road. Well, there he was, just as big as daylight, you know. But going that fast, you can't just turn the motorcycle too fast, you know. And so he was kind of shot, shot. Well, I thought, well, if he just keeps going, I'll cut him behind him. And by the time I thought that, he stopped. And of course, with the motorcycle, he'd look at anything and run right into it. And of course, I seen that dog stopping, I looked at him as the dog, see if he was going to move or not. Boy, if you're looking at anything on the motorcycle, you run into it. You go where you look. Well, I hit that dog. Oh, boy. Well, it's the same dog that was laying out there about 50 feet away from the road, where they found him. But that motorcycle went right up in the air, just like we'd gone in orbit. And I remember getting off of it. And then that's how I remember it. I was down on track. Well, there was about five or six railroads across this crossing, switching about tracks. And then, but for the crossing, they had planks laying there. And they was nailed down, big spikes. Well, dry weather, the bikes were sticking about that high on the planks. And anyway, I was going ahead of that motorcycle, and I was on my butt just going around and around. And the motorcycle behind me going around and around, and we just went clear across there. And right at the end of the depot is where we all come to a stop. Well, he was still straddling the motorcycle yet. Oh, he got his leg all chewed up pretty bad. He tripped up quite a lot. But I wasn't hurt anything. I felt all right. I lifted the motorcycle off of him and bought that type of car stop. It was going to take us up to the hospital. Yeah, the hospital didn't pop that sound, the little hospital. And so I helped him off with the motorcycle, set up and off of him. And they stopped the car there and started losing it, and I got in the back seat. And my guys, I didn't much want to hit the back seat until I passed out. Well, I didn't wake up until he got up at the hospital. Well, my folks lived about a block above the hospital there. So they was getting him out of the car and getting him in the hospital. And they wanted to know if I was going in. I said, no, I'm going home. Because all I'd done was really outside shaking the episode. I just sprung my wrist. Some way or other I got my wrist sprung. Otherwise I wasn't hurt a bit. I was just shaking up. Anyway, I went home and the pencils had tore off of me. I got them changed and ate a little dinner, didn't I? I kind of lost my appetite. And I went back down. I walked back down to Cross Country as a mill. And they was up there. I don't know. They had all the stories going around. The bull that was killed and what was killed and the other one. I don't know what the heck. Anyway, I come walking up the stairs and they saw me on that group. They turned around and thought they'd seen a ghost coming. Boss, he wanted to know, you come back to go to work? I said, what the devil's man come down here for if you ain't going to work? Oh, boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This wasn't the same boy that all these other things happened with, was it? This wasn't the same boy that all those other things happened with.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, no. That's another guy. He was an older man than I was. But no, that was the only time he got the record with me. Once or another, I don't think he ever rode with me anymore. Once or another. Oh, and then you hear tales, you know. I don't know. They had tales. They'd tell my boy about when my escapades were that motorcycle. Anyway, the old potluck store, you don't remember it before it burned down, do you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I knew it was there, but...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, it was a big... Well, it was a regular department store. Hardware, jewelry, and merchandise of all kinds. Regular department store. Anyway, they had big plate glass windows out in front next to the highway they were going down through. Anyway, I heard the story Frank told me. Somebody told him by a guy's how I went through them plate glass windows and motorcycle. I had trouble talking about that. It wasn't me. Oh, they get exaggerated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were saying that it was you that did that.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, everything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were saying it was you that did that.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, they tell me how I went through the potluck windows. Motorcycle, well, I never heard of anybody going through the window. I don't know if my son ever believed me or not, anyway. I denied that one. I told him no, that didn't happen.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had other troubles, though, besides?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, that was the worst one. I had a car down there one time. I was going at night. Going down the potluck. You know how you go down that hill there? Well, you know about halfway down the hill there's a concrete wall that goes up the street? Goes up the other way there, there's a concrete wall. Well, I was going down the hill and a car came up and it hadn't frozen me and turned up that street. Well, I never thought about nothing, but it wasn't very far from the road them days to that wall. Well, I was going along there and about that time I thought everything was clear. Never thought about nothing. Here comes that car backing out on the highway. Backed right out, even frozen me. And, man, I slammed on the brake and the car came out here on the street and turned up this way. Well, first, I might have went straight ahead. I could have missed him, but I tried to turn, keep hitting him. And he kept on backing. So, anyway, I had the motor leaned over this way and I was sliding and I slid into the side of the car. And I broke my foot up a little bit. And that was about all the damage done to me. But it sure rims back my bike. I didn't know what twisted the frame was. And it never did run right after that. You know, when you get the frame twisted, the wheels don't crack. You go down the road straight, one wheel run off the back, one wheel run off to one side a little bit. They just don't handle the loop that way. So I was a little bit leery riding, so I just quit riding.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And tell me about your hell raisin' up in Harvard with the motorcycle.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, boy, you'll never forget nothing. Oh, another kid. Now, we had a, two of us, our fathers got brand new Harley-Davidson's. They were 74s, same year. We used to ride together a lot, raising heads. Always up at Harvard. Like this was back in the hayseed country, and that was back a little further. Bunch of hayseeds up there. So we rode up there in a little soft brick pot up there. We went in and got us a pop. When we come back out toward the bike, there was a whole bunch of them hayseeds around, laughing and making fun of them. And one big old guy weighed about 300 pounds there. Well, he says, one thing about it, he says, if you can't ride it where you want to go, you can pack it. I said, yeah. So I just took my bike and laid it over on the side. I told him, now you set that back up on the Palouse. You just didn't do it right. You could take it by a handlebar in the street, and it would move ahead. It goes up easy. But just try to lift it, you know. I mean, it weighed over 600 pounds. Well, you got to hold a handlebar, and you're going to lift it up, and the wheel of the bike run ahead. He was all over the street, and the bike had him down half the time. Finally, I told him, well, you give up? I said, why do you think you want to pack that thing? Anyway, they was giving us a lot of hayseeds up there. There was a kind of ballpark down the lower end of the street there. You know what Harvard is. Down there around toward the old depot. They used to use it for ballparks. So we got started. We went down in that ballpark and turned around and cut it back up. There they all just got across the street. Jim and I was about half mad at anybody about it. I thought they were horsing us. So I told Jim I was going to go scatter them batches. So we cut loose. And that guy that was coming pretty quickly just fell apart. We went through. They just fell apart by a guy like that. We went through there about 50, 60 miles an hour. I told him about that. Went on back home. I told him I was going to scatter them batches. So we cut loose both of them side by side going through there. We didn't slow down. They waited until the last minute. And they just went like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So there was pretty good rivalry between Harvard and Princeton kids.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh yeah. Almost as bad as dog patches. Old McCoys and that. Oh heck. You got that thing running?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, it's just about giving out now.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You can shut it off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I want to know about the rivalry between Princeton and Harvard.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, you really have to get somebody older than I am. Because I was just a kid and that was going on. I don't know. There used to be rivalry between Potlatch and Palouse. They used to play ball games among themselves. A lot of rivalry both ways there. Princeton and Harvard. They used to play Harvard one Sunday. And then Harvard would come down here and play the next. Oh, I don't know. Oh, there's some people in a bunch that has to raise the devil. Have a fist fight or two maybe out of it. A couple women get into it and have a hair pulling match. About an awful lot of them out into it. Used to be one girl over here at Princeton. Married a woman up at Harvard. They used to tangle. Of all the language you ever heard, they could roll it out. I learned words there I never knew was in a book.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be at a ball game or something like that?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, it'd be ball games. A woman lived at Harvard and won that girl down here. They used to have verbal contests. That's about what mine was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did Princeton have a rivalry with Potlatch or was it just with Harvard?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, just Harvard. Harvard and Blues used to have it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, Potlatch and Blues.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Blues, yeah. Blues and Potlatch. Yeah, they used to have their rivalries. That all faded out after years. Same as Harvard and Princeton. They all growed up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the Princeton kids, they stuck, would stick together and the Harvard kids would stick together.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, more or less that way. Well, I married a Harvard girl, so I guess it wasn't too bad. Yeah, and she was raised up at Harvard. I had a bunch of my grandkids over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Mm-hmm. Would you tell me the story about play hooking going up to Harvard? I like that one.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah. I'm kidding. Somebody made a crack about it. I was play hooking up to Harvard. The train comes through Princeton just about noon hour, you know, going up the line. So all we are, he's all for it. So we grabbed our case by a guy and went over there and the train was pulling out. Why, we took after it on the tail end of the catch-on to it. And by the guys, I caught on and he just couldn't quite make it. From the time I found out he couldn't make it, the train was going too fast, I was afraid to get off. So I rode on to Harvard all by myself. And I went across a bunch of kids up there to Harvard that was downtown at noon hour. So they played hooking and we all went skating up there just above the tail of the waves of our pod. We got to play with hockey and one thing or another. By the time the train flew, well at three o'clock the train was going to come back down again, you know. And my guys said we was out there skating all by and somebody heard the whistle. The train coming down the grade up there at Yale, at Yale grade. Man, I jumped those skates off. You know, after you went off the skates a while, you know, your feet feel funny walking. You know, you gave us some ice skates. Oh boy, it felt like I had clubs on my feet for legs. Anyway, I took off down the track and the train caught it with me and I had to get off and wait until it went by. And then I took after it again and it went on down deep on the top. And by the guys, I just runned, I didn't think I could run anymore. It just seemed like I couldn't get in the wind. But I managed to make it because the train started to pull out, I caught on the back of it and went back home. I had been ten steps behind, you know, even as it pulled out, I couldn't have picked up speed enough to, you know, I just pooped. I just couldn't have put on the steam enough to caught up with it. I just caught it, just about to seem like a butterfly. I had to take two more steps, I couldn't have made it. I might have been up against walking about six miles down the track. But I just didn't make it. Got back down and the teacher never missed me. I was in the car and the devil was supposed to go with me. He was laughing. I said, well, the teacher never missed me. So I got by with that slick as a whistle. He was quite a kid, that Glenn Thomas. Where'd Anna go? You what? Huh? Where'd Anna go? With your wife?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, she's still here.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: She is. She must be on notice or something. Maybe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of school? Did you like school pretty well or was it sort of a nuisance to you?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, I don't know. Some of it went violent. It's just like, I'm from a kind of like my guys. The little thing the guy has to say on television every morning. He has a new one every morning. One of them yesterday was that, as long as we have final examinations in schools, we'll always have religion. Prayer. We'll always have prayer. As long as we have final examinations in school, we'll always have prayer. Prayer. That's about where school was with me. No, I didn't mind school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I went to school where it was just supposed to be so much of it. You know, I just never finished. You just keep going and going and going.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You never get through with it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I am now, but it sure took a long time.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You go through college too. Well, back in my days, very few of them went to college. People couldn't afford it. People couldn't afford it. It's been well to raise the kids without sending them to college. Keep three meals on the table a day. That's about all the expected of parents some days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how your parents came by this place? Came by the place that they got here? Oh yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, they bought it in three different parcels. The first place they bought was up where I was raised up on the hill up there. That was a hundred and fifty acres. He bought that from people named Admerson's. Owned it before he got a hold of it. And the guy that lived here had his name was Pete Ireland. And before him was a triple, a personal triple in Elgin. That bunk they lived here to start with. And Ireland lived here. Dad bought eighty acres back here off of this place. And then later, it was forty acres left again. So when the old folks about had it, they had to sell this. So we then bought this forty and that put the hundred and twenty together here. That's the way he acquired this. That's the way he come by it. He bought it in three different pieces. Well, the old fella lives here, I don't know. His boy got sick and had pneumonia. Tired and had the consumption. And them days they couldn't do much about it. So they spent money on him. And they just finally had to sell that eighty off of it to keep it going. And they later got down to where they had to sell this. Or they were going to lose it. They had a mortgage for all it was worth and they were going to lose it. So my dad could take it off their hands. That's the way he come by this.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: So I bought the whole works. And then I had the, it was going to be a hundred and sixty acres over on the mountain over here. Timber claim. Lady lost her husband over there and she was going to let that go back. So I went and investigated. I had to pay the back taxes on it. Oh, it seemed like hundreds or more dollars. And I picked up that claim. And I kept it. I don't know how it was for ten years and I never did find out where it was. When I was looking for it. Could never find it. Finally I needed it to find. And after he got it, he got, oh he went and got one of the head guys. Scalers down at the mill. Lime cruisers. Timber cruisers. And he went up and located it. And after he located it, I was up there several times and looked at it. And I did it to him. And I bought another hundred and twenty acres up here. But he sold it now. That hundred and sixty, that timber claim, he sold that. And I bought a hundred and twenty acres from Potlatch. And I needed that to him. That's where he got his house built now on that. So I got the hundred and sixty and hundred and twenty left. I was going to sell a hundred and sixty out of that. As soon as I got the logs off of it. A hundred and sixty up there. As soon as I got the log, I got the timber already sold. I suppose the log at this winter. As soon as that's logged off, I'm going to put that up for sale and get rid of it. And, I don't know what the hell to do with this. Keep it a guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's your home.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, this is home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There are guys that were around here at homesteading that really stood out as being characters to you. And there are guys that stood out as being real characters.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Real old homesteaders?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, of the homesteaders. And when you were a kid.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, I'll tell you, when I was a kid, there were a lot of old-timers coming here and home-toods way early. And when I was a kid, I used to go up around up here in the country and there'd be abandoned cabins, you know. homesteaders and homesteaders and pulled out left. There used to be all kinds of cabins up here in the woods. Guys would go in and take claims out and prove up on them and leave. Most of them sold out the Potlatch. The mills, I mean, most of them sold out the Potlatch. Oh no, there used to be a lot of cabins. I used to, kids used to be woodwrights. You know what woodwrights are? Old thubbers. Used to be a lot of them up in the woods and they'd be in them cabins. Those kids used to ride in one cabin and another and chase wood rats. Didn't have nothing else to do with that. Who was homesteaders? I don't know. Real old homesteaders. I just don't know who was homesteaders and who wasn't. I think Butterfield's and Cochran's and Bud. I believe they were all homesteaders.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't even mean just homesteaders. I meant, you know, among the old-timers. You were guys that stood out as being characters. You were guys that sort of had, you know, their own quaint ways of doing things, you know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, I think they got a little bit ahead of me there. I wasn't bored quite quick enough for that. All the time I got to be a kid big enough to, you know, to... The Indians was all out of the country and everything would settle down. Yeah, there was too much excitement I can remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you start working in the mill?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: When I started working down there. After World War I. Yeah, 1919.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they start you doing? Wages. No, what was your work?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, I don't know. I think I started out. First job was picking engines. You know, they'd edge the boards and you'd step back on the road and pick the engines off. That was my first job and then I got up to help an edgerman. And I edged a little bit. The carriage was bouncing back up there at the front of the mill. They interacted with me so I had my sights on them. So finally I got on the carriage and dogging and I learned to set. Set ratchets. And then I wasn't happy yet until I got a hold of the main throttle on the carriage. Run that carriage back up. Finally wound up setting. Took the start at the bottom. Worked right up the line. Oh, I didn't get a steady job. I saw a night shift, you know. And then on a run day, well, in the old side you saw a night shift. I saw a light ratchet. I saw a night shift. And then to laugh I had a steady job. I was getting, well, kind of come to a crossroads. Usually the day I had a chance to take the farm over. And, uh, either that or a state each sawdust. And I just decided by, I guess I know, that $8 a day was pretty good wages them days, you know. $3.40 was just regular work. But nevertheless, I made my choice and I quit. Come and start farming. And a lot of people say I made a heck of a mistake. I should have stayed with a mill, but... God damn it, I don't know. I never seen no millionaires ever come out of that damn mill. I didn't get to be a millionaire farmer neither, but... You're kind of working for yourself. You don't have to have a boss looking down your neck all the time. When you get through doing a day's work you can look back out and see what you've done. Down the saw mill you just look up the log deck and it's full of logs. You work on that deck all day and you go back the next morning and it's still full of logs. It's just like... I don't know. It doesn't seem like you get anywhere. I suppose I probably should look at the boards going out until the logs come in. I don't know, but it is that way, you know. You just go down there and you beat on logs all day. And when you get through at night the log deck is still full of logs. You go back the next morning and there they are. It's like you haven't done nothing. You couldn't see where you've been any place. Only thing, if you didn't put out enough lumber you'd hear about it. Didn't do a good job you'd hear about it. I don't know. It just seemed like... Like old Terry C. Ernie used to sing that song. How the heck would it go? Another day of work, other than death. Working for the company store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of skill did... What was made a good sawyer? What did you have to do to be a good sawyer? What kind of skill did you get from working there?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You just learn from experience. You start out like this sawyer's got to go to the back house where you take his place. Saw for five or ten minutes every day. Pump out the rig, pump the water out of the steam lines before the mill starts in the morning. You just get out and finally buy a guy's maybe. You get pretty good. Maybe the sawyer will get a bellyache and have to stay home a day or two from work. You get the saw day or two. And finally you get good enough and start an electric shift. They'll give you a job as saw.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What makes a good sawyer instead of a bad sawyer as far as the technique?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, it's combinations. You've got to learn the grade lumber. You have a blackboard across the track coming up there and they want so many thousand feet or five quarter, number two common. Select, whatever it is. You've got to know when you come to that in the log so you can cut that auger out. Of course now they don't really saw for a grade anymore. They mostly saw by guys just to get chips because a chip may not load. They just waste lumber or something fierce. They just don't make 2x4s, 2x6s, stuff like that. But otherwise they... And then they haven't got the log. Now they used to really grade the log out like they used to. They used to get a big yellow pine and white pine log. Well, you take a tree and there's always a limb around the south side of the tree. And the other side is pretty well bare. You can always look at the woods. You don't know what direction to go while you look at the tree and where the heavy limbs are. The south side of the tree. That way you can know where they go north or south. It's kind of like a compass. Well, when they saw them in the log, they come in the mill, you've got a log by a guy that around this part of the log here will be all knots. Well, if you turn the log out, you can see it looking within the log. Maybe there will be a... This part out here won't be a knot in that part of the log, you see. Well, you turn that log, lift the ends of the log against the blocks, and you start sawing on the log here and you saw in and you get... Oh, you saw way into that log and you get absolutely clear lumber. No knots. But then you turn it and that would be all in a select lumber. You select a select. And that sells for about maybe $250,000. Where the rest of this log over here would be just box lumber and shop and just common lumber. But you get all the money off the log that first cut. And then you take and get the cheaper lumber on the rest of it. But if you don't know it now, you want to know good sawing. That's what a good sawing does. You just turn that log so you get all this like lumber. Like my saw here, that I worked for. He'd turn the log there and turn it over and maybe have this just hump up here like this and just down here would be knots. Well, he'd saw in here and have part of that board would be select and this part down here would be knots. And just, you know, that's... Well, you see, that's... Up in there, they tell you a guy knows how to get the cut out of a log. It's judgment. Well, you have judgment and no one. You can take a look at the end of the log when you load it. What this will call it, select by the log is on the outside. And you saw that select all through the thing before you ever turn the log. And, of course, then when you get in the small log, there ain't much grading to do. You just sawing boards. Well, then you got to be fast. You can't mess around turning the logs. Taking a half hour to load the log and get good enough you can run that darn thing. That carried back and forth. I'd never stop it. Turn the logs on the fly and load them on the fly. Rig never comes to a stop. You don't believe that, then?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I don't believe it. But it sounds like you got to be real good to do that.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, take practice. Practice makes it perfect. Oh, no, I got... I'm not bragging. I got good enough at like pumping the rig out. You have bumpers on both ends. Say the track is, say, 40 feet long. You got them bumpers on each end. The end of the track, you know. So if you reach over it, they got them air cushions in them. But I got good enough one time I could pump that rig out wide open. Go down that end and see when you're on the steam feed. It ain't like a mechanical is. You open the steam up and the rig will go this way. That's what they call push feed. You open the steam up and the rig will go this way. But you can't wait to get the other end before you pull it back. When that rig is picked up feed, you got to pull it back and the rig will go close. And then it will come back the other way. Then you got to catch it before you get the other end. Let it go and get the cushion. It's running on the piston. What they call the shotgun feed is a big piston. They're all 12-inch cylinder. But like your carriage is going this way, while you open the valve, you got this valve back here open. And it's shoving the carriage this way. Then when you get ready to come back, while you open this valve and the steam will pour in this end and drive the piston back. That's the way she goes. It's back and forth. But anyway, you get it going wide open both ways. It's just going back and forth. Well, before it gets to the other end, you got to, your rig is going that way but you reverse. Well, the rig will just go up there and click the bumpers. And then when it goes down the other end, you cut it over and you click the bumpers. Like you just click, click, click, click. You never hit the, you got about that much cushion. And then the bumpers fly back in solid. It's just a solid base but kind of a, it was an air compression chamber back there. It had a little hole for that to come out. So when the carriage hit it, it would cushion it, see. And I got, so I just pumped that rig out wide open and just clicked them bumpers on each end every time. And, no, no, no, it's like turning your log. You know, like, well, your log next here and here you are and the carriage running in front of you. Well, when you run out and kick the two boards off, you come back wide open, you'd tuck these log loaders, you'd tuck the log roll before the carriage got back there. But the log would be rolling down onto the carriage. The rig would still be coming back but you'd have a time before the rig got back there because you had to hit it with a nigger and never stopped the rig. Just keep it going. And then turn the logs. You had three blocks sitting there, like this, going back and forth. Well, you'd come back wide open, turn the log, you'd catch the log back there, and hook it in between these two, see, you'd hit, slam it up. You'd hook it here and it's like, there's the carriage is going. And that's what they call turning them on the fly. And then if you, real good, well, you could go back by gush and hook the log here and hit it here. That's what they call hooking them in the tail. But most of the guys, they run back, I don't know my size, they'd never do that. They'd have to run straight back past these two blocks and hook it here and hit it. All that one nigger. When you're going back, you know, them blocks hang out there right in the road of your nigger. You got to run your nigger up out of the hole, catch the log and get it back before this block hits it. See, that's a, if you didn't get the nigger back, this block would hit the nigger. So you got your three logs there, your log, you got these blocks going. You're coming back to the wide open hook log here and drop the nigger back and then log them to the hole and you just, that goes through the blocks. That makes a half turn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what's the difference between hooking on the fly and hooking on the run?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, that's the same thing on the fly, that's what they call them is on the fly. But if you hook them in between the back blocks, see these back blocks are about that far apart. And the next block is probably ten feet apart. Just like these, the way I hear these, ten foot here and this is only about three or four feet here. They ain't got much room to work in there. Carry's coming back there just like that over the top of it, you know. You catch it right here and then push it up between these two blocks. The rig's going this way all the time you're doing it. Never stops. Never stops. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they were pretty, management was pretty tight about what they wanted accomplished. They wanted a lot of work turned out.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh yeah, I don't care how fast, how much longer you saw, they were always harder for more and more. You made a good collision. They wanted the last ounce.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well who gave the orders, was there a foreman?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, yeah, they had a foreman. And you know they had to please the foreman, my guys, for getting them cut out. But they had to please the lumber graders and the check-scalers and everybody on down the line. And you know something was wrong, I could hear about it. So they had to slow down the line. Time to pull the lumber load on the bus car and ready to ship out. Everything went wrong was the sawyer's fault.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you're telling me that you had to take some gaff? You got people complaining about this and that to you?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh yeah, yeah, you'd have to take so much of it. Maybe it wasn't your fault, maybe there were four sawyers and then they had poor carriages. Well one guy could lallygag and the other guys go like heck. But that guy lallygagging, if he was a friend of the boss or something, he'd get away with it. Well then the whole bunch of sawyers got blamed because the cut was down.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, one guy going to lallygag by a guy that doesn't know how to do nothing. You'd have the other three carriages be batten away doing the best they could. But still that one guy would bring your cut down. Most sawyers don't nowadays work for a day's pay. The whole sawyer I used to work for. The guy would say he'd lallygag along and get a big log and he'd just feed it like he was going to break that saw. He'd step in ratches for him, you know, he'd step down the street by a guy and say, Well, you can go half asleep waiting for you to get a board off. Anyways, at the time he'd drip the rig off under me one time, but I didn't see the superintendent come down in the mill. I guess he looked back and seen the superintendent come there, just he went out to cut. I guess he just dripped that rig wide open right quick and, damn, he lost me off the cage. I wasn't expecting it. Because he'd seen a superintendent stand there. Boy, you'd take to see them guys come around because he really couldn't have saw passed. He'd just drip that rig around top of Pierce and them guys around, you know, just drip you around. Really, you don't have to go past to drip the rig around. Just like riding a cradle, you still go past, you know, like I say, you just put it out there and just move it right back and this way and turn the log and everything. This guy, he'd have to run back there and he'd drip the rig around and get the log turned, you know. Well, you're sitting on that seat on the carriage and you're just going like this. Used to wear you out. Well, you couldn't feed the saw steady. You'd have to keep what they call pump. Bigger go like this. You'd just keep humping through the diamond cut. And you'd do that. Yeah, like every other son of a miss would go kind of pumping through. Anyway, I think I should know if you do it smooth. There's nothing to it. You just get the rig going that way and just pull the lever over there and just keep feeding it through. And when you get ready to come back, you come back the same way. You'd keep it just going like this, you know. Pumping.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how'd the man get by?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Something never found out. Couldn't saw himself out of the back house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Out of a box house, you say?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Out of a back house. Couldn't saw himself out of a back house. Well, guys, he got by for years sawing down there. Anyway, he finally got retired. There's a little formula up here at Bovill. And they needed to saw you right back so somebody told them about Wheelo. Come down and get him. Well, he never sawed any place but on that rig. Like I say, I don't know how he got by. But he went up there and sawed. He didn't last long, just a little bit. The boss up there took a look at him about twice the way he sawed. And he come down, the boss come down and wanted to get me to go up. So that's after I quit. And of course I started farming. He wanted me to go up and saw him. I said, no. I just can't be two plays at once. I need all my time down here. I wouldn't have mind trying it just for the heck. I went up there with him. Like I say, you can't farm bad guys since you sawed Mill Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your job setting ratchets amount to when you were sitting on a carriage setting ratchets?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, you're due all to make an olympia. You got the blocks, there's three blocks that the log lays against. And if you want to make an eight inch can, you know, first place you go back a little log, you size the log up and pull the block back about the size of a miniserio. Call you for a half inch in order to take a slab off. And then he'll tell you eight, eight, eight, eight ole inch. Well, you turn the log over and you come out with eight and a quarter that's the width of the log, the thickness of the log. Can't. I reckon you can 12 inches or six inches or four inches. But that's one by 12, one by six, one by eight. You know, one by fours. And if you want two by fours, when you sawing a log up by a setter, you have to set them like you got a log so thick here you're cutting boards off of them. Well, the saw takes out an eighth of an inch. And you got to figure out, you got what you call the boardroom. Maybe you got 30 inches out here, but he turns it over to last cut all the way up. And then he'll tell you what size canter. If he wants a five quarter, six quarter, two inch, maybe three inch, whatever he wants. Well, you got a ratchet bar there you can put down. What they call a Johnson bar. You put it down what you want. Cut off four inches, three inches, three and a half, whatever it is you got your notches. You put it down that way and all you got to do is pull the lever back and that much goes out. Well, there's steam and air set.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you're supposed to estimate how, you're supposed to figure it out so that you don't get waste on that log.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Really the setter's got a more scientific job than Brian Sawyer has, really. Yeah, it's up to him if the board lever comes out thin, why it's your fault. You got all the combinations you got to keep in the back of your head. I couldn't keep nothing back there now. I used to be able to keep the difference in combinations. But, oh like you know you got a log and you take the slab off, the face off of it. He motions you on 12 and a half by a guy's two five quarters. Well, you know you got your saw cut coming out and you got to know. So you can take them two five quarters off when he calls for them. He'll hold up that five quarters, and you hold that up and you take them two off. And still you got to be on track so you can take two more boards off and still come out 12 and a half inches. See what I mean? Otherwise you might, it makes everybody mad if you come out there with a half inch by a guy's off and you have to motion on them. Sorry everybody, I got a shave or something. Take off a half inch slab off of there. And that damn little half inch goes out through the mill for everybody to see. It's a good thing. So, you don't make many mistakes twice. I don't know one time, I ain't bragging about it, but I was only in the center down there by a guy's. I got an order from Jap. Jap Ann wanted what they called Japanese squares. I think there was three and five eighths, but two and a half or something. I forget. But the way you do it, now that was really something to figure out when you get a big log. And they'd motion for all Jap squares, you know, whatever it was. You'd make it this way, three and a half, three and five eighths, and then the other way, two and a half, I think it was. Well then when they got the log all squared up, you'd start making them. Well, say you had it laid down so you had to set it at three and five eighths, and you still had a log with an eighth and eighth of a saw cut on each one of them. But you'd have to have it figured out here, it was a third across this log. Well you set it up this way, then saw that, not saw it clear off, you know, leave it to hang about so much on the tail end. Then they'd pull that back, then you set it up that much more, then saw that off and let that hang. And you'd have a whole bunch of them out here hanging. Well, when you got down to the block, that had to be just exactly two and five eighths, right there. And by a guy's, they were really watching them awful close, because the Japs were pretty particular people. It don't make me too many mistakes as hell. So anyway, then you come back and you turn that whole bunch over, very gently within the nigger. Then you'd start taking two and a half inches off the other way, and every time you'd cut off, there'd be a whole bunch of them Jap squares going down the line. And next time you'd cut off a bunch, and there'd be another one right behind them, just a whole bunch of them little squares. And so that guy's, you know, that really took some hot sweat. Depending on the size of log, you know, there's no two logs the same size, you'd have to figure it out. And I don't know. I fell out of that job, I guess, every time they'd get a log to make Jap squares, they'd throw it over on our rig and I had to sell them. And they didn't pay a noise for nothing extra for these.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they use those cuttings for? Did you ever know?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I used them for ram rods for guns that they were going to shoot back at us. I don't know what the hell they used them for. I don't know what the hell they used them for. They just had that order. So many thousand of them Jap squares they wanted to ship over Japan. I never did know what they used them for, but they had to be precise. That guy on the green chain down there, out on the planter by the guys, they'd put the ruler, the measure on every damn one of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is a green chain?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Green chain? Well, that's where the lumber goes out of the sawmill. It goes through the edges and kicks the edges off the boards. And they're ripped or whatever they want to know. They take a 1x12 come down, they want to rip it and make 1x6s where they can rip it there at the edge. Then they go down and drop on the trimmer table.
