And when your saw logs out in the woods, you make them a little longer, like a 16 foot log, probably 16 to 3 inches. And then if they got a bad butt on them or something, you know, when it goes through the trimmers, one of the saws come up and they cut them ends off. Usually it's a 16 foot board, but they cut them down and spoiled the ends off of them. And then they go from trimmer on down into the green chain. And that's a big long chain out through the mill there. And then they cut it. They only got a bunch of lumber piles. And that's where the 2x4s go on one pile, 1x6s on another pile, 1x12s on another pile. And they have a whole bunch of guys on both sides of the green chain pulling lumber off on different piles. And that's what they call a green chain. That's the green lumber goes down there, you see, and then it's loaded there and taken over to the dry kilts, put through the dry kilts and dried out. And that's what goes through the dry kilts and they send it to the planer. They plane them, whatever machine work they got to do on it over there. They make flooring and steering and siding, whatever they do is done over in the planer. Yeah, quite an operation. And then it goes from the planer to the bus cars. And they ship it. Oh yeah, the planers are quite a machine. They can set them up to make any tongue and groove or shiplap, you know. And they get her set up and make everything just right. Then they get back and the boat goes through there just one way or another. And they get everyone exactly like, whether they make a shiplap or tongue and groove. They get all the heads picked up on it so they just measure out this right. They turn the juice on her and away they go. One board just zing, zing, zing, come out of that damn thing like shotguns. You ever see a big planer like that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I can imagine what it would be like though.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: That noisy? Oh boy, them saws. Them old grander heads. Sure noisy. Yeah, I was going to work around one of them high speed machines and one of the ear plugs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the fellows that worked in the mill stick together pretty much? Did they have much to say to each other? Was it a pretty close working bunch?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, they piled up pretty good. Oh, they're just like any group of men. Some men nobody wants anything to do with them. Some men buy guys. Everybody wants to kiss them. Probably takes his pants down. I don't know. It's just general human nature. Yeah, they're just like human, I guess, as any other labor force.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you think that the guys feel about the management? Management? Yeah. How do you think that, was the feeling of management pretty hostile or pretty...

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, the management was all right. I don't know. Of course, you get better superintendents when people like better than the other one. It isn't the management that raises heck. It's the dang gone two-bit bosses, straw bosses. You know, you give a guy a little authority and he's got to know how to push their weight around. And they're the ones that cause trouble. It's the bosses, not the management. You know, if you've got any grievance to take to the management, you can usually get a ear to listen to your side of the story anyway. But it's the bosses that raise the devil around the plant, not the management so much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say bosses, you mean like the foreman and guys like that?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, foreman and straw bosses. You give a lot of guys that just can't stand a little authority. Got to shove their weight around and give them a little authority and they've got to try to push their weight around. And they're the ones that, you know, they've got their pets and people they like working on them and the ones they pick on and the ones they don't. You know, that's how it is. It's the dang bosses that cause all the friction around the plant. I think it works that way. There's a saw mill or a mine or a dome mill. I don't know where it is. A lot of people always say the management, the management's having a boss. It wasn't the men when they started the mill down here, when the brick mill started out 12 hours a day. It wasn't the men that asked for that, but the management told them what to do. And they didn't have no unions or nothing. They'd just either take it or get the heck it going. Well, the stuff like that was management. But otherwise, I don't know. You get a group of men together, right?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you used to get yourself in hot water at all? Seems like you got some pretty strong ideas about things. I just wondered if you ever got trouble for being outspoken.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, but that's a long story. Yeah, that's a long story. And I don't think I ought to put it on tape. But I went out. Yeah, it come to a case where I got either... That was mixed up with the management over the boss. I took my troubles to the management and come down to where one of us was going to be fired. Either the boss or me. I told them that was all right with me. You just get to the bottom. I was all okay. I wound up buying them the boss to get me the bottom.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Huh. Well, I wouldn't want you to use these, but what was the matter? What was the guy doing at all?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, it all started out with the boy's son. That boss's son. He had a son down there. He got trying to push me around, push me to quit so his son could get my job, see. And I just decided I guess he wasn't going to do it. So I fought back and got to the point where I went to the management. That's about all there is to it. The management said, well, he's going to get to the bottom of the racket and find out what's the matter. I told them that's fine with me. As long as you buy a gush, don't go down top to the boss and get his idea and then come back and can me. If I turn out wrong, okay. If I guess if he's wrong, buy a gush. I still don't want you to find out. So he said he would. Well, everything went along all right. So I was up there one day and he called me up to the office, the main office. I thought, well, here's it. The way it was going down the line. Well, the way things were going, I didn't care too much. So he said, well, he says, I figured out who's the fault. He said that Swafford was the boss's name. I said, he's the fault and I'm going to have to get rid of him. I said, I'm sorry. I said, anybody's got to lose their job over it. I told him my story of it and I said, by gosh, I don't think I've been done right either. So anyway, it went on for quite a while. I didn't think anything was going to happen. So one day that whistle blew and I tuned the manager up in the middle, talking to the boss. But then he went down before the whistle blew, went down there and went outside. So we got ready to go out in the middle. The old boss was sitting there with tears running down his cheeks. I kind of took a gab rather than kind of guess what happened. Sure enough, you guys wasn't busy. Well, it was his fault. He just kept riding me all the time. There was nothing wrong with my work. It was just that kid of his. I was holding him kid back. You get rid of me, you get my job and everything will be happy. Hey, Anna!

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anna?

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: No, I was going out for some matches. I was right on the corner of the table and she goes through the door.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Got some matches over. I have good matches.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: What's the matter with her?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She doesn't like the matches.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: She doesn't like matches?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She doesn't like these matches. Oh, oh, oh. Because you have to turn them over, you know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, the brothers on the back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You have to turn them over to strike them and all that thing.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Where did Brighton and Dennet finally go?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Oh, they went home.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, they went home.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: See, he's got to eat and be ready to go to work. But he gets off at four in the morning instead of four thirty. Four. But by the time he gets home, it's four thirty. And that's what she was meeting him.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh. Well, what's he been doing all this time? I haven't seen nothing of him.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, you know.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I'm told, for example, that we've been in the slavery papers for him.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: That was his first week on that shift. Huh? It was his first week and you know how he always was resting. Yeah. Changing shifts and then trying to get his sleeping schedule changed. It's bad.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I told him we've been in the slavery papers for him. He said we're going to stop and get him.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And he's having trouble now with that wood and boards up there in the cleaner. You know, he gets the rash from the lumber.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, right. The rash.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Like the ... Potlatch. I don't know what you call it. A wood rash or something.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, sawdust. Sawdust.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I don't know.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And she's got next week and then she's through with all her parties.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, that'd be something.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Next Thursday Friday or something.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: What you going to go and do next? Go plumbing up in Thailand?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, she says that she's going to get busy and sew a while. Stuff like that.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Two bits of rain tack in there?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No, no. I turned the coffee pot on.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, that's what the coffee pot is.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I'm not sure if you guys warned me or not, but I turned it on to warm it up. Two bits.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, Anna, come over here and sit down. The tape's around here.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Well, oh, is it running? Yeah.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: You can't think of it, but just give me a hack like you do and take it down on tape.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, give it to them, man. I'd like to hear it. Well, I didn't know that. Now I got my own tape.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah. Where'd you take the tape at?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I couldn't think of anything to say. She asked me, you know, what about my grandparents, you know, in Spangle? Uh-huh. And there's lots of interesting things, you know, you can think of, but not at the time you want to. Uh-huh. No, you couldn't think of anything. She told me to write down some things if I wanted to, you know, and then I would know what I wanted to say. Well, Laura said that she wished that she didn't have to go because she wished that she'd been able to talk to you more this morning. Because she thought that as the two of you talked, you were really warming up to talking about it. Both of you were. So she said that she had to, she felt badly about having to leave. How long did you and your grandmother go to school? Long, long, long years ago. I don't know how many, hundreds. My grandfather, Philadelphia Dutch, Pennsylvania Dutch. Anyway, he went back and he brought out two big bells. You know, those great big old bells, one for the church and one for the school. And he brought them back to Spangle. And the last time I heard, they were still... Still there. That's nice. My grandpa, though, I don't, I think he was just a big... He used to go uptown and bring all of them, four bums or kids or whatnot, you know, down from the hotel.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: See her, her grandma went to the hotel there, Spangle.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Tell her grandma to fill them up.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: What was he done? He had 80 acres out of Spangle. And some guy, when they was freightin' in the way he traded out 80 acres.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: No. That was in that other place, on those Paha Flats. What is the name of those flats? I got it written down. Anyway, he had a farm out there that was long before they moved to Spangle. Well, two or three of these kids got sick and died and they were buried over there. Well, then it was too hard for them to stay, so he turned that little land over to a friend of his. He'd take care of the grave of the kids.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Oh, but that either existed outside of Spangle. Well, some guy friend of his comes through there and, anyway, he'd come up from the river, had flowers. He traded out 80 acres for so many types of flowers.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Yeah, but he was, he got it cheap, you know. Spangle wasn't anything to make up for it.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Well, he had to do this. He got there and sat down on the stump and sat there for 15 minutes at the jorges, something like that. Anyway, he got it cheap and sold it to you. Something like three cycle flowers or something.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: But they had the haulers stuck there from Walla Walla. There was no town in Spokane in the face of the coast. They had the haulers by wagon from Walla Walla when my grandparents first landed in Spangle. There was an old bachelor there. No, he wasn't an old bachelor. There was a family that lived there by the name of Spangle, and that's where the town got its name. But the house I was born in is still out there. It was 50 years ago.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Spangle was named after a guy that lived there, Spangle. About like Mowo up here.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And my grandfather was the second man there. He was a carpenter, so he built houses and wanted them. But it's easier for him to give his grandma to work than, you know, shouldn't have been that way.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: He showed you his boss anyway. He showed you his boss.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: Oh, he's swell up they say. He could bring somebody in and say, fill him up. He's pretty big hearted.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Yeah, well, like Deary up here. He was named after an old potluck manager down here, old Bill Deary. And Bovill was named after an old, well, old homesteader, I'd say Bovill. He was built in the cabin there, so when they built the town, they named it Bovill. I'd say, yeah.

speaker: Anna Utt
words: And now only my people that left the Spangle are the ones in the cemetery. And that's pretty full of, on both sides of the house. There's no one living there, or laid anymore, I don't think.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: Old-timer, I guess I told you I was the first common old-timer, 93 years old, that died down in Cottonwood. His granddad moved in then his dad, and still kids and grandkids all lived around Cottonwood, except one sister, I think, lives in Clarkson. I think that's the way it was, wasn't it?

speaker: Anna Utt
words: I didn't hear you.

speaker: Emmett Utt
words: I'd say that old fella down in Cottonwood, 93 years old died, his granddad moved in there, and then he came in there with his dad. And the whole family and grandkids and everything's all around Cottonwood yet, except one sister, I think, lives in Clarkson. Boy, if you've got among them about 10 years ago, you could really get some old-timer stuff.
